dimension: Under-built for production posture (authn/z, validation, idempotency, observability, logging/redaction, secrets, deploy/install/repair/rollback, migration safety)
summary: Partially covered: idempotency, schema gates, and secrets-in-Markdown scanning are strong, but the architecture DRAFT omits or under-builds several production controls that its own companion artifacts (THREAT_MODEL, RISKS, REQ-NF-001) require — most critically worker-API caller authentication and a structured-logging/redaction pipeline.

findings.finding: The renderer->worker loopback API has no specified caller authentication. The architecture draft repeatedly says the worker API 'binds loopback only' but never specifies a session token, origin allowlist, or per-launch shared secret that the renderer must present. Loopback binding is NOT authentication: any local process, and a malicious visited web page via DNS-rebinding/CSRF, can reach a 127.0.0.1 tRPC port that exposes raw workspace data and side-effecting commands. THREAT_MODEL.md and RISKS.md both assume this control exists; the binding architecture drops it.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §5 ('Worker API binds loopback only and exposes typed commands/queries/events') and §10 ('Worker API is loopback-only') — no auth mentioned; vs THREAT_MODEL.md line 22 (controls = 'loopback binding, origin/session checks, typed tRPC') and RISKS.md RISK-005 ('loopback API auth/origin checks')
findings.proposed_fix: Add to §5/§10 a renderer->worker authentication requirement: a per-launch session secret minted by Electron main, injected via preload, and required on every tRPC call + event-stream subscription; plus an Origin/Host header allowlist to defeat DNS-rebinding. State that unauthenticated loopback callers are rejected.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress / §10 Local App API

findings.finding: No structured-logging or log-redaction architecture. The threat model lists 'log redaction' as the mitigation that keeps secrets and raw workspace content out of logs, but the architecture draft has no logging/telemetry section, no redaction policy, and no statement that provider prompts / raw Employer Work content / AgentResult.logs are excluded or scrubbed from logs. Provider prompts carry raw confidential content; unredacted logs are an uncontrolled egress/leak surface that bypasses the §16.5 egress gate.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md has no logging section (§9.9/§12 cover System Health UI only); THREAT_MODEL.md line 41 ('Secrets written to Markdown or logs' -> mitigation 'log redaction'); PRD §13.1 AgentResult.logs and prompt fields can carry raw content
findings.proposed_fix: Add an observability/logging subsystem section: structured logs with correlation/workflow-run IDs, a mandatory redaction layer that strips credential-shaped strings and raw-content fields before any log sink, an explicit rule that prompts/raw payloads are never logged at default level, and that logs honor the same workspace egress policy as providers.

findings.finding: Install doctor / repair / app-upgrade / rollback path is under-built. The draft §13 covers only first-run clean install; §4 says a migration mismatch 'reports repair instructions' but no repair/doctor command is architected, and there is no app version-upgrade data-migration path or rollback story. REQ-NF-001 explicitly puts 'deploy/rollback' in scope, RISKS.md RISK-013 names a 'doctor command', and PRD §16.1 names an 'install doctor'.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13 (clean install only) and §4 ('Migration mismatch: startup blocks worker and reports repair instructions'); REQUIREMENTS.md REQ-NF-001 ('deploy/rollback'); RISKS.md RISK-013 ('doctor command'); RISK-009 fallback 'Manual resume/health repair path'; PRD §16.1 'install doctor'
findings.proposed_fix: Add to §13 a doctor/repair command (verifies prerequisites, DB/migration state, Temporal/GBrain reachability, Keychain access, connector auth, lease/lock health and recovers stale locks) and an app-upgrade/rollback subsection covering version-pinned runtime bumps (PRD §17 Maintainability), operational-DB migration on upgrade, and rollback/downgrade behavior.

findings.finding: Migration safety for the operational store is thin: no backup-before-migrate, no forward-only/transactional migration policy, and no operational-DB backup/recovery strategy. The operational store holds approvals, outboxes, connector cursors, and audit — losing it on a failed migration or file corruption loses in-flight external-side-effect state, yet (unlike Markdown, which is Git-backed) it has no named backup path. Drift between SQLite/Postgres migrations is only covered by contract tests, not a safety policy.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §4 ('Migration mismatch: startup blocks worker'); DATA_MODEL.md 'Migration and Compatibility Rules' (lines 108-115, Drizzle tested on both dialects only); §4 lists outboxes/approvals/audit as operational-store contents
findings.proposed_fix: Specify in §4/§13: forward-only, transactional Drizzle migrations with a pre-migration backup/snapshot of the operational store, a recovery path if migration fails mid-flight, and a stated backup/restore strategy for the operational SQLite file (approvals/outbox/audit) parallel to Markdown's Git backup.

findings.finding: SecretsPort / KeychainSecretsAdapter is absent from the architecture draft's component model and repo scaffold. The draft only asserts negatively that the store 'does not store secrets; Keychain does' (§4) but never specs the secrets abstraction, the key-retrieval flow into providers/connectors, or a package home — even though provider keys and OAuth tokens are top-listed assets and PRD §7.3 defines SecretsPort.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §4 boundary line and §17 repo scaffold (no secrets package/port); vs PRD §7.3 ('SecretsPort -> KeychainSecretsAdapter'); THREAT_MODEL.md assets ('Provider API keys and connector OAuth tokens'); REQUIREMENTS.md REQ-S-003
findings.proposed_fix: Add SecretsPort/KeychainSecretsAdapter to the §5 security architecture and the §17 scaffold (e.g., packages/policy or packages/secrets); describe how worker retrieves keys at use-time, that keys never enter renderer/logs, and the dev-only .env non-secret boundary.

findings.finding: Renderer rendering of untrusted content and Content-Security-Policy is not addressed. The renderer displays meeting transcripts, source text, and assistant notes — all untrusted/imported content — so XSS in the renderer is the exact path that turns the missing worker-API auth (finding 1) into raw-data exfiltration. The draft's Electron-security list (context isolation, no Node integration, narrow preload) does not include a CSP or a safe untrusted-content/markdown rendering requirement.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §5 'Electron security' bullets (no CSP, no untrusted-render rule); §11 surfaces render Ingestion Inbox / meeting notes / Copilot output; THREAT_MODEL.md line 21 failure mode 'XSS invokes privileged operation'
findings.proposed_fix: Add to §5/§11 a strict renderer Content-Security-Policy (no remote script, no eval) and a requirement that imported/untrusted content is rendered through a sanitizing markdown pipeline, never as raw HTML.

findings.finding: The worker API is described as 'typed tRPC / typed commands' but typed is compile-time only; runtime input validation on commands crossing the untrusted renderer boundary is not stated. The strong JSON-Schema discipline applied to provider output is not mirrored on the renderer->worker command boundary.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §10 ('Use tRPC for TypeScript-native commands and queries'); §3 validation rules cover model output/external writes but not API command inputs
findings.proposed_fix: Add a §3/§10 rule: every worker API command and query input is validated at runtime (e.g., zod schemas on the tRPC procedures) and rejected on failure, since the renderer is an untrusted boundary.

findings.finding: Observability is specified as a UI surface but not as an emitting subsystem. PRD OBS-1/OBS-2 enumerate the exact System Health metrics and the distinct, persistent, audit-linked failure items required, but the draft only lists 'System Health' as a §11 UI surface and a §12 test; no architecture component is named that collects/emits these metrics and failure items.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §11 (System Health surface) and §12 (tests) only; PRD §9.9 OBS-1/OBS-2 (connector status, GBrain health, workflow runs, queue depth, failed write-throughs, sync lag, cost, approval backlog; distinct persistent failure items)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a brief observability/health subsystem in §4 or a new section mapping each OBS-1/OBS-2 metric and failure class to the worker component that emits it and to the read model the §11 surface consumes.

findings.finding: Encryption-at-rest of the operational store is unspecified. The operational SQLite DB holds raw cached payloads (RET-1), audit before/after summaries, source-envelope content, and event content — confidential Employer Work data that is NOT in Keychain and NOT Git-backed. ASM-009 locks 'macOS session + Keychain sufficient' for AUTH but does not address payload-at-rest encryption. Is FileVault the assumed control, or should the app encrypt the operational store (e.g., SQLCipher) given Employer Work confidentiality requirements?
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md line 18 (only secrets are Keychain-protected; payloads not addressed); PRD §9.10 RET-1 (assistant-held raw payloads cached up to 30 days); ASM-009 (auth only); §4 operational-store contents
findings.proposed_fix: Owner decision: (a) rely on FileVault full-disk encryption as the documented at-rest control and state that assumption in the architecture + install docs, or (b) require app-level encryption of the operational store (e.g., SQLCipher) and Temporal/local DB files. Do not invent the answer.
